hero: We can read speech straight from the muscles of your face — no microphone, no sound. When you talk, your muscles give off the faintest flicker of electricity on the skin, and we can read that flicker back into the words you meant to say. This is how.
muscles: Speech begins as movement. To say even a single word, your brain fires nerves that drive dozens of muscles across the face, jaw, and throat. And here is the key: every time one of those muscles contracts, even slightly, it gives off a tiny pulse of electricity.
articulation: Watch the muscles light up as the face speaks. The orbicularis oris rounds the lips. The zygomaticus pulls the cheeks. The masseter drives the jaw, the genioglossus moves the tongue, and deep in the throat, the sternohyoid sets the pitch. Every sound is a different pattern of dozens of muscles, firing in sequence.
electricity: Each of those contractions sends a faint electrical signal rippling out to the surface of the skin. It is thousandths of a volt — far too weak for you to feel. But rest a sensitive sensor against the skin, and it can listen in.
sensors: So we place a grid of 31 sensors on the face and neck. Each one sits over a different set of muscles and measures the electricity passing beneath it — and it does this 5,000 times every second.
recording: And this is what comes back: 31 wiggly lines, thousands of points a second, overlapping and full of noise. Hidden somewhere inside this tangle is everything you tried to say. Now we have to pull the words back out.
features-window: First, we slice the signal up in time. A small window, just 25 milliseconds wide, slides along all 31 traces, and every 20 milliseconds it takes a snapshot. The endless stream becomes a steady 50 snapshots a second — like the frames of a film.
two-sensors-dancing: The next stage hinges on a phrase: which sensors move together. Let's make it concrete with just two. Picture two dancers. When one steps up, does the other step up with it? If they always move as a pair, they move together. If one rises while the other falls, they move against. And notice — this has nothing to do with how loud either one is. It is purely the dance between them.
features-cov: Now, what tells two sounds apart? Not how loud any single sensor is, but which sensors move together. So for every snapshot, we compare each pair of sensors, filling a 31 by 31 grid. Watch: two different gestures light up two completely different patterns.
every-pair-grid: Now run that same test for every pair, not just two — thirty-one sensors, each checked against all thirty-one, including itself. Lay every answer in a grid, row by column. Each tiny cell is the together-ness of one pair. The diagonal, where a sensor meets itself, is just its own size; it is the off-diagonal cells that carry the real story of who moved with whom. One snapshot becomes one whole grid.
features-embed: That grid is huge and noisy, so we steady it — blending it mostly with itself, and a little with a plain, average pattern. Then we funnel it down to just 384 numbers: one compact fingerprint per snapshot. And the whole sentence becomes a filmstrip of fingerprints. This is what the network actually reads.
what-is-a-net: We keep saying the network reads these fingerprints. So what is a network? Think of it as a function — a deep stack of layers, filled with millions of tiny dials, that turns numbers going in into a guess coming out. Nobody writes the rules by hand. At the start the dials are random, so the guess is nonsense. Everything it will ever know lives in how those dials end up set. Next, we will see how they get set.
how-training-works: Training is simpler than it sounds. Show the box an example whose answer you already know. It guesses; you measure how wrong it is — call that the error. Now picture the error as the height of a hill, and the dials as the position on it. Each step rolls a little downhill, in the direction that makes the error smaller. Show another example, step again. Do this millions of times, and the dials settle in the valley. That settling is all training is.
targets-phon: What should the model predict? Not letters — English spelling lies. Though, tough, through. So instead, the word cat becomes its sounds. We predict the roughly 40 building blocks that linguists call phonemes, so the model can spell words it has never seen.
targets-units: Phonemes are a little coarse. So beside them, we add a finer set: 100 sound-shapes that another model discovered entirely on its own, from thousands of hours of audio — plus a single blank symbol, meaning nothing new here yet. Two views of every sound teach it far more than one.
where-units-from: Those hundred finer sounds — who decided on them? Nobody. Here is the trick, called self-supervised learning. Take a model, play it thousands of hours of plain audio, and hide little chunks. Its only job is to guess what was hidden from what surrounds it. To get good at that, it has to quietly sort sounds into categories of its own — this kind of hiss, that kind of hum. We never named them. It found them. Those recurring shapes became the hundred units.
two-answer-keys: So now the reader studies against two answer keys at once. The forty phonemes are clean and meaningful, but coarse — the names humans gave to sounds. The hundred units are finer, but stranger — texture the machine found itself. Why both? Because being right on two different keys is far harder to fake than being right on one. It is like revising from two sets of notes: the overlap forces real understanding. And neither key, mind you, says when each sound happens.
alignment-problem: Before any of this can train, face the puzzle head on. Each second is fifty snapshots, but a short word like cat is only three sounds. Imagine a movie's subtitles with every timestamp erased — you still know the words, and their order, you just don't know which frame each belongs to. That is exactly our situation: we know the sounds of the sentence, never which snapshot is which. So the model can't be told the timing. It has to discover it.
ctc: But nobody ever labelled which snapshot is which sound. So the model guesses one for every frame — a sound, or a blank. Then we collapse it: merge the repeats, drop the blanks, and the word appears. And many different timings all fold down to the same word — so it learns the order, without ever being told the timing.
ctc-many-timings: So how does it learn from a puzzle with no answer key? Here is the trick you just watched. Instead of betting on one true timing, it considers every timing that would spell the right word — and adds up their odds. Training simply pushes that total higher. Get the order right, however you stretch or squeeze the sounds, and you are rewarded. No single timing is ever chosen as the truth. They all win together, and the order sorts itself out.
encoder-bidir: To name the sound at one instant, it helps to see its neighbours. The original model could only look backward — which is perfect for live captions. But for a finished recording, letting it look both ways at once, before and after, is simply better.
what-is-attention: Looking both ways was the gentle version. Here is the real tool. Stand on one frame and shine a beam at every other frame in the sentence, near and far. Each one answers with a weight: most say barely, a few say a lot. The frame then updates itself mostly from the few that matter, ignoring the rest. That reach-everywhere, weigh-by-relevance move is called attention — and distance simply does not matter to it.
what-is-convolution: Attention is great at the far view, but it can smear the fine grain. So the reader carries a second lens: a little magnifier that slides along, looking only at a frame and its close neighbours. It catches the sharp local texture — a quick onset, a tiny edge — that the wide view blurs flat. Reach for context, magnify for detail. A reader that runs both lenses at once is what the next stage already called the Conformer.
memorize-trap: You might expect a bigger reader to be better. Here it is the opposite. We have data from a single person — a lot, but finite. Pour that into a giant model and it just memorizes the exact examples, then crumbles on anything new, like a student who crammed the answer key instead of learning the subject. So the reader is kept deliberately small — only four layers — small enough that it has to learn the pattern, not the answers.
conformer: The reader uses two lenses at the same time. Attention reaches across the whole sentence to pull in context from anywhere; a small sliding window reads the fine local detail. And we keep it deliberately small — just a few layers — because with only one person's data, a giant model would simply memorize.
heads: From here, the reader splits into three outputs. The 100 fine units. The 40 phonemes that everything downstream relies on. And a third — a stream of 1,024 numbers that isn't for reading text at all. Its only job is to imitate a real human voice. Here is why that matters.
copy-the-teacher: That third output exists for a trick called knowledge distillation. Normally you only tell a student if its final answer is right or wrong. But a strong teacher knows more than the answer — it forms a rich internal picture of what it sees. So let the student copy that picture directly, like laying tracing paper over a master's brushstroke and matching its whole shape. The student inherits detail it could never have found from labels alone.
voice-as-teacher: So who is the teacher? The real voice. While the data was recorded, a microphone ran beside the sensors, just for this. A powerful audio model — one that already listened to an ocean of speech — turns that voice into a rich picture, deep in its ninth layer. The muscle model's third output is trained to copy that picture. And here is the catch worth holding onto: this mainly sharpens the sounds it hears, not the words yet. Then, at real use, the microphone is gone. Only the lesson stays.
distillation: During training, and only during training, we also recorded the real voice. A powerful audio model listens to it, and we pull the muscle model's third output to match what it hears — a teacher to copy. When the system is actually used, there is no microphone. Only the lesson remains.
frozen-recognizer: But just sounding close to the voice can cheat — two different sounds can blur into one vague average. So we add a lock: a frozen sound-reader that the model cannot change. It forces those copied features to actually spell out the right sounds, pushing the blurred ones back apart.
blurry-average-cheat: Why bolt a locked reader onto that copy? Because matching has a sneaky loophole. The student copies one long string of numbers. Suppose two sounds sit close together. The laziest way to look near both, on average, is to plant a single mushy point right between them. The copy-score looks great. But that point is neither sound — a smooth blur that scores well and means nothing. The two sounds you needed to tell apart have quietly melted into one.
frozen-lock-how: The fix is a reader the student cannot bend. We bolt a sound-reader onto the copied features and freeze it solid — its dials are locked. Its one job is to check that real, distinct sounds can actually be read out of the copy. Since the student can't soften this reader, the mushy blur now fails outright. To pass, it must pull the two sounds back apart and make them genuinely decodable — close to the voice and still spelling the right sounds.
twin-warmstart: Rather than start from nothing, the new reader copies what an older model already learned. Then we train a second, tougher twin under much harder conditions — hiding moments, dropping sensors, adding noise and rotation. The two end up making different mistakes — and that is exactly why averaging them helps.
second-opinion: Remember the tougher twin that fails differently. Here is the payoff, and it is a second opinion. If two doctors make the very same mistakes, the second adds nothing. But if they err in different places, averaging them lets the errors cancel where they disagree, while the truth they share survives. That is why the twin was trained under harsher conditions — not to be better, but to be different. Different mistakes are the asset. Average them, and the noise washes out.
wfst: Sounds still are not words. So we search one enormous map — how 34,000 words are pronounced, woven together with how English words tend to follow each other — and we light up the single cheapest path through it. That is five times more words than we trained on, so it can spell out ones it has never seen.
sounds-to-words-search: Turning a stream of sounds into words is a search problem — the kind you already love. Picture an enormous road map. Every way the sounds could become real words is a route, and every step carries a toll: cheap when the sounds and the grammar agree, expensive when they fight. A whole sentence is one route across the map, and its cost is the sum of its tolls. So reading a sentence becomes finding the single cheapest path. Computers are very good at that.
knobs: The very same sounds can be ice cream, or I scream. Two dials decide which. One sets how much to trust the muscle reading against plain English, landing near 0.25. The other curbs the model's habit of guessing blank, near 2.0. Set them wrong, and almost every word comes out wrong.
what-is-lm: Part of that map's toll comes from a language model, so let's pin it down. A language model has read mountains of text and learned one thing well: given the words so far, which word likely comes next. Say peanut, and butter lights up bright while bicycle stays dim. It cannot hear a thing. It just knows, from sheer exposure, how English tends to flow — making natural sentences cheap and gibberish expensive. The map here uses a small one; a far bigger one shows up later to choose.
ensemble-nbest: Now we combine. Averaging the two readers steadies the signal, to about 20 percent wrong. And instead of a single answer, we keep a ranked list of dozens of candidates, pooled across the dial settings. A perfect chooser picking from this pool would be wrong just 9 percent of the time — and closing that gap is the prize.
what-is-wer: We keep saying twenty percent wrong, eighteen percent wrong — measured how? It is called word error rate. Line your guessed sentence up against the true one, like grading a dictation. Count every fix you would need: each word to swap, each one to add, each one to delete. Add them, divide by how many words were truly spoken. Say two edits in an eight-word sentence — that is a quarter wrong. Lower is better, and the whole project is a fight to push it down.
rerank: So we add a chooser: a 7 billion parameter language model, squeezed small, that reads the top candidates and the detected sounds together, then picks the sentence that makes the most sense. And we audited it carefully: it invents nothing the evidence does not support.
binding-constraint: One honest catch before we tally the climb. The chooser is brilliant, but it can only pick from the list it is handed. For the hardest errors, the right word was never in the sounds at all — so no language model on earth could reach it. That is why we level off near eighteen and a half. The sound-error, stuck around twenty-one percent, is the true ceiling. Breaking below it would need sharper ears, not a smarter chooser.
journey: So here is the whole journey, left to right. You speak. 31 sensors listen. The signal becomes fingerprints. The reader turns them into sounds. The map turns sounds into a pool of likely sentences. And the chooser picks the words you meant. Everything you have just seen, in order.
arc: And it pays off, in three moves. First, fixing how sounds are decoded into words — 51 percent down to about 40. Then, looking both ways while copying that teacher voice — down to 26. And finally, combining the readers and adding the chooser — down to 18 and a half. Notice the sound-error fell sharply on the second move — it truly heard better — but barely moved on the last.
approach: To be clear about what is new. Starting from a published system that was 51 percent wrong, we changed three things. A retuned, open-vocabulary decode. A full-context reader, taught by the parallel voice during training. And a two-model ensemble, pooled and reranked by a 7 billion parameter language model. Together: 51 percent error, down to 18 and a half.
silent: So what is it for? Silent speech. You simply mouth the words, and it types them — no sound at all. Dictate in a quiet room. Speak privately in a crowd. Get a message through where a microphone never could. Or go completely hands-free. Trained on a single healthy speaker, this is a silent keyboard, not a medical device — and using it across many people is the work still ahead.
foundation: None of this begins without the team at U C Davis. The recording corpus, the 31-channel sensor array, the geometric features, and the very first E M G to text decoder are all theirs — led by Harshavardhana Gowda, with Daniel Comstock and Lee Miller. Our pipeline is just one small block resting on that foundation. We only built upward.
credits: And we invented none of the pieces. The Conformer, WavLM, the HuBERT units, the C T C training, the front-end, the cross-modal distillation, the reranking, the word-map decoder, the lexicon, Qwen, and QLoRA — every one came from someone else's published work. This is composition, not invention.
thanks: Thank you for making it all the way to the end. This was made by Varshith Madishetty, built with the help of Claude.
